title: Deduplicate EdDSA verification in ModelInstanceBinding via shared credential commitment
description: ModelInstanceBinding runs two full EdDSAPoseidonVerifier instances (~6k constraints each) over the same credentialCommitment. The operator signature and provider signature share the Poseidon5 credential hash computation but each EdDSA verifier internally re-derives intermediate values. Extract the shared BabyJub scalar multiplication setup into a common subcircuit and feed both verifiers from a single Poseidon5 output wire. This saves ~2k constraints (~6% of the 35k budget) and reduces witness generation time proportionally.

title: Reduce AgentPolicy Merkle depth from 20 to 16 with configurable template parameter
description: AgentPolicy uses MAX_DEPTH=20 (matching HumanUniqueness), but the agent tree will have orders of magnitude fewer leaves than the human tree. A depth-16 tree supports 65k agents — more than sufficient for years. Each Merkle level costs ~500 constraints (Poseidon2 hash). Dropping from 20 to 16 saves ~2k constraints and cuts witness generation by ~8%. The template parameter already exists; this is a compile-time and trusted-setup change with matching verifier contract regeneration.

title: Batch human+agent proof verification into a single PLONK proof via proof aggregation
description: Every handshake requires two independent on-chain verifyProof calls (human + agent), costing ~500k gas total. Implement a thin aggregation circuit that takes the two Groth16 proofs as private inputs, verifies both inside a single PLONK circuit, and outputs a single proof. This halves on-chain verification cost to one pairing check (~250k gas). Use snarkjs's existing Groth16-in-PLONK recursive verifier template. The aggregation circuit adds ~100k constraints but proving with rapidsnark stays under 2 seconds.

title: Replace bit-decomposition range checks with single IsZero on overflow witness
description: AgentPolicy and Delegation each use Num2Bits(64) for range-checking permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, and currentTimestamp — each decomposition costs 64 constraints. For fields that are already decomposed elsewhere (permissionBitmask bits are individually accessed for scope checking), the range check is redundant. Remove the duplicate Num2Bits(64) for permissionBitmask in AgentPolicy since the bitwise scope check already decomposes all 64 bits. This saves 64 constraints per circuit instance and eliminates redundant witness computation.

title: Replace JS witness generation with C++ witness calculator for 5-10x speedup
description: Currently witness generation uses the snarkjs JS-based witness calculator, which is the bottleneck for HumanUniqueness (BabyPbk scalar multiplication is expensive in JS). Circom 2.1.6 supports compiling witness calculators to C++ via --c flag. Build C++ witness calculators for all three core circuits and integrate them into the SDK's proving pipeline alongside rapidsnark. This should reduce end-to-end proving time from ~1.5s to ~0.5s for HumanUniqueness on commodity hardware, making interactive handshakes viable.

title: Define application/bolyra+cbor proof envelope media type
description: The protocol lacks a canonical serialization format for proof payloads exchanged between parties. Define an application/bolyra+cbor media type with a CBOR envelope containing: proof type discriminator (human/agent/delegation), public signals array, proof bytes (a/b/c points), and a version field. Register with IANA per RFC 6838. This enables content negotiation in HTTP-based handshakes and prevents ad-hoc JSON serialization divergence across SDK implementations.

title: Formalize did:bolyra resolution algorithm with DID Core conformance
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md exists but lacks the required resolution algorithm per W3C DID Core §7.1. Define the concrete steps: parse did:bolyra:<commitment>, query IdentityRegistry for enrollment status and current Merkle root, construct the DID Document with verificationMethod (BabyJubJub key for humans, EdDSA operator key for agents), and authentication/assertionMethod relationships. Include the CRUD operations matrix (Create=enroll, Read=resolve, Update=N/A for humans, Deactivate=revoke). Without this, no universal resolver can implement did:bolyra.

title: Publish deterministic test vectors for all three circuits
description: Create a spec/test-vectors/ directory with JSON files containing known-good inputs, expected public signals, and expected verification results for each circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation). Include negative cases: expired credential, stale root, scope superset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation. Pin the BN254 curve parameters and Poseidon round constants explicitly. Any independent implementation must be able to reproduce identical outputs from these vectors to claim conformance.

title: Add humanTree root history buffer matching agentTree's 30-root buffer
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but not for humanTree. Any new human enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight proofs. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 circular buffer to humanTree. This is a correctness issue that also blocks interop: any relayer or verifier holding a proof generated seconds before an enrollment will see InvalidHumanProof. The IETF draft must specify root acceptance windows for both trees identically.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent brute-force
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) in AgentPolicy.circom. The permissionBitmask is only 8 meaningful bits (256 values), so an observer can brute-force the scope from the public scopeCommitment by trying all 256 masks against known credential commitments. Change to Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a private input. Update the Delegation circuit's chain-linking to pass the salt through. This is a spec-level privacy fix that must be coordinated across circuits, contracts, and the IETF draft.

title: LangChain BolyraCredentialTool with automatic scope-gated tool access
description: Build a LangChain BaseTool subclass that wraps createAgentCredential() and proveHandshake(). The tool should accept a permission bitmask in its schema and automatically gate downstream tool invocations — e.g., a FINANCIAL_SMALL agent can call a payment tool but not a PII-access tool. Return structured ToolOutput with proof artifacts so LangChain's agent executor can pass them to the next tool in the chain. Ship as `bolyra-langchain` on PyPI with a 5-line quickstart in README.

title: CrewAI task delegation → Bolyra Delegation circuit mapping
description: CrewAI's Crew.kickoff() delegates tasks from manager to worker agents. Build a CrewAI callback/hook that intercepts task delegation events and generates a Bolyra Delegation proof with scope narrowed to only the permissions the sub-task requires. The worker agent carries a cryptographic proof that its scope is a strict subset of the manager's. This maps directly to the existing iterative Delegation circuit (max 3 hops = manager → specialist → sub-specialist). Publish as `bolyra-crewai` on PyPI.

title: Off-chain session token for multi-step agent chains
description: LangChain/CrewAI agent chains make 10-50 tool calls per task. Requiring an on-chain handshake per call is prohibitively slow and expensive. Add an SDK-level session token: one on-chain verifyHandshake() mints a signed session token (JWT with proof digest + expiry + scope), and subsequent tool calls verify the token off-chain via the SDK's verifySessionToken(). The on-chain contract gets a batchCheckpoint(bytes32 sessionRoot) for periodic anchoring. This is the single biggest adoption blocker for framework users.

title: AutoGen GroupChat mutual auth middleware
description: AutoGen's GroupChat pattern has N agents communicating peer-to-peer. Build middleware that establishes pairwise Bolyra handshakes when agents join a group chat, using the sessionNonce as the group-chat ID. Each agent proves its credential once on join; the middleware caches proof artifacts and exposes a is_authenticated(agent_name) predicate that AutoGen's speaker selection can check. This prevents unauthorized agents from injecting messages into a group chat — a real attack vector in multi-agent systems.

title: Standard proof envelope with application/bolyra+json content type
description: Framework integrations all need to serialize/deserialize proof payloads, but each will invent its own format without a standard envelope. Define a JSON schema for BolyraProofEnvelope: {version, circuit, publicSignals[], proof{pi_a, pi_b, pi_c}, metadata{prover, timestamp, chainId}}. Register application/bolyra+json as the content type. All SDK methods (TS and Python) should accept and return this envelope. This prevents every framework adapter from reinventing serialization and makes proofs portable across LangChain↔CrewAI↔AutoGen pipelines.

title: Nullifier domain separation across circuit types
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy as Poseidon2(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce), and Delegation as Poseidon2(delegatorCredCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, sessionNonce). These share the same Poseidon hash without a domain separator tag, meaning a collision between a human nullifier and an agent nullifier is theoretically possible if scope happens to equal a credentialCommitment and secret equals a sessionNonce. Add a per-circuit domain tag as the first Poseidon input (e.g., Poseidon3(DOMAIN_HUMAN, scope, secret)) to make cross-circuit nullifier collision structurally impossible. Write a formal property proving distinctness of nullifier domains.

title: Prove merkleProofLength bounds are fully constrained
description: All three circuits pass merkleProofLength as a private input to BinaryMerkleRoot. If BinaryMerkleRoot does not internally range-check that depth <= MAX_DEPTH, a prover could supply depth=0 and get the leaf itself accepted as the root, bypassing tree membership entirely. Write a formal invariant property asserting that for any valid witness where merkleProofLength < 1 or > MAX_DEPTH, the constraint system is unsatisfiable. Verify against the @zk-kit/binary-merkle-root implementation. If the check is missing, add an explicit RangeCheck inside each circuit.

title: Prove expiry > currentTimestamp ordering is tight
description: AgentPolicy uses LessThan(64) to check currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp, and Delegation checks delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry and currentTimestamp < delegateeExpiry. These comparisons use circomlib's LessThan which is correct only when both inputs fit in the specified bit-width. The Num2Bits(64) range checks exist but are on separate signals — write a formal property proving that for every satisfying witness assignment, the actual field element ordering matches the intended uint64 ordering. Specifically verify there is no witness where both range checks pass but LessThan produces the wrong boolean due to signal routing or intermediate overflow.

title: End-to-end scope monotonicity proof across delegation chain
description: The Delegation circuit enforces delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0 per hop, but there is no formal proof that composing N hops preserves monotonicity end-to-end (i.e., that the final delegatee's scope is a subset of the original delegator's scope). Write a formal inductive proof: base case (hop 0 scope ⊆ AgentPolicy scope via scopeCommitment binding), inductive step (if hop k scope ⊆ hop k-1 scope, then hop k+1 scope ⊆ hop k scope). Encode as machine-checkable properties using either Lean 4 or as Circom witness-generation test vectors covering 1-, 2-, and 3-hop chains with attempted scope escalation at each hop.

title: Prove cumulative bit encoding covers all implication edges
description: The circuits enforce bit4 → bits 2,3 and bit3 → bit2 for the permission bitmask, but the spec lists 8 permission bits (0-7). Formally enumerate all required implication edges from the spec and verify the circuit constraints match exactly — no missing edges, no spurious edges. Produce a truth table of all 256 possible 8-bit values, classify each as valid/invalid per spec, then generate witness inputs for each and confirm the circuit accepts exactly the valid set. This catches both under-constraining (accepting invalid encodings) and over-constraining (rejecting valid ones).

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment. Implement a CrossChainRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of the humanTree and agentTree roots from the canonical chain (Base) and validates them against the L1 state root available via each L2's precompile (ArbSys.arbBlockHash on Arbitrum, StateSender on Polygon). This eliminates reliance on trusted relayers — any party can relay a root update by submitting the storage proof. The relay contract maintains the same 30-root history buffer as the primary registry.

title: Bind chain ID into sessionNonce to prevent cross-chain proof replay
description: The current sessionNonce is chain-agnostic: a valid handshake proof generated for Base can be replayed on Arbitrum or Polygon if both chains share the same Merkle roots. Fix this by defining sessionNonce = Poseidon2(verifierNonce, chainId) and requiring the contract to enforce block.chainid matches the chainId embedded in the nonce. This is a single-line change in HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits (add a chainId public input, bind it into the nonce hash), plus a require(chainId == block.chainid) in IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake().

title: Cross-chain nullifier deduplication via shared Merkle accumulator
description: When the same identity operates across Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon, each chain maintains an independent usedNonces mapping. A sophisticated attacker could use the same nullifierHash on two chains within the root-relay latency window. Add a NullifierAccumulator contract on L1 that aggregates nullifier commitments from all L2s via their native bridges (Arbitrum Outbox, Polygon FxChild). Each L2 registry checks the L1 accumulator root before accepting a proof, with a grace period for in-flight proofs. This closes the multi-chain double-spend window.

title: Chain-specific verifier deployment with calldata-optimized proof encoding
description: Arbitrum and Polygon have different gas cost profiles than Base (Arbitrum charges heavily for calldata via L1 posting, Polygon is compute-bound). Create chain-optimized proof submission paths: on Arbitrum, compress proof elements using point compression (halving calldata from 256 to 128 bytes per G1 point) with an on-chain decompressor; on Polygon, batch multiple handshake verifications into a single transaction using a MultiVerify wrapper that amortizes the pairing check. Add deployment scripts and Hardhat configs for arbitrumSepolia and polygonAmoy testnets alongside the existing baseSepolia.

title: Portable delegation chains across L2s
description: A delegation chain started on Base (hop 0 → hop 1) should be continuable on Arbitrum (hop 2) without re-proving prior hops. Extend the Delegation circuit's previousScopeCommitment to accept a cross-chain attestation: a tuple (sourceChainId, scopeCommitment, storageProof) that the contract verifies against the source chain's registry state. Add a delegationChainOrigin field to the on-chain DelegationVerified event and track it in lastScopeCommitment so the hop counter is globally consistent via the L1 nullifier accumulator from proposal above.

title: Off-chain session token with on-chain checkpoint batching
description: After a successful handshake, emit a signed session token (JWT with ZKP receipt) that allows subsequent agent actions off-chain for a configurable TTL (e.g., 5 minutes). Batch session token roots into a single on-chain Merkle checkpoint at intervals. This eliminates per-action gas costs, making Bolyra viable for high-frequency agentic commerce (tool calls, API chains) where paying gas per handshake kills adoption. Implement as a new SessionCheckpoint.sol contract and SDK method `createSessionToken()` / `batchCheckpoint()`.

title: Payment-bound scope proofs for agentic commerce
description: Extend the AgentPolicy circuit with an optional paymentCommitment public output that binds a specific payment intent (amount, recipient, currency hash) to the permission proof. This lets an agent prove 'I am authorized to spend up to $50 on behalf of Alice for this specific merchant' in a single ZKP, replacing the current pattern where permission check and payment authorization are separate trust boundaries. Critical for the 10-year trajectory where agents autonomously transact — without payment binding, permissions are decoupled from actual spend actions.

title: Human tree root history buffer (parity with agent tree)
description: The agentTree has a 30-root history buffer preventing proof staleness during concurrent enrollments, but humanTree has none — any new human enrollment instantly invalidates all in-flight human proofs. Add the same `uint256[30] humanRootHistory` ring buffer to IdentityRegistry.sol and update `verifyHandshake` to check membership in the buffer. Without this, production deployments with >1 human enrolling concurrently will see random handshake failures, which is a dealbreaker for any real adoption.

title: Five-line hello-world that proves adoption readiness
description: Create a `@bolyra/quickstart` package (or add to existing SDK) that bundles a pre-compiled circuit WASM + zkey, an in-memory Merkle tree, and a single `bolyra.demo()` function that runs a complete human-agent handshake in 5 lines of code with zero setup. Ship it as `npx create-bolyra-demo`. The current onboarding requires compiling circuits (~2 min), downloading ptau, and understanding Merkle trees before seeing a proof — that's a 30-minute cliff that kills developer conversion. Every successful dev protocol (Stripe, Firebase, Clerk) has a <60-second 'aha' moment.

title: Verifiable delegation receipt chain for audit trails
description: Add a DelegationReceipt event log that emits a structured receipt per delegation hop: (hopIndex, scopeCommitment, delegationNullifier, timestamp, delegateeAgentRoot). Aggregate these into an off-chain receipt chain (JSON array of receipts with back-pointers) that any party can independently verify against on-chain events. Enterprise buyers (banks, fintechs) won't adopt agent delegation without audit trails — this makes every permission narrowing step independently verifiable for compliance, without revealing the actual scope bits (preserving ZK privacy).

title: HumanUniqueness proofs go stale instantly — no root history buffer
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has NO equivalent for humanTree. Any new human enrollment changes the Merkle root, instantly invalidating every in-flight HumanUniqueness proof. In a handshake, the human proof is generated first; if a single enrollment lands between proof generation and on-chain verification, the proof reverts with an implicit root mismatch. Fix: add a `humanRootHistory[30]` ring buffer identical to `agentRootHistory`, updated on every `enrollHuman()`, and check `humanMerkleRoot` against the buffer in `verifyHandshake()`.

title: scopeCommitment is brute-forceable — missing blinding salt
description: In AgentPolicy, `scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment)`. The permissionBitmask is only 8 meaningful bits (256 values). An observer can precompute Poseidon(mask, credCmt) for all 256 masks against a known credentialCommitment (public from the Merkle leaf) and recover the agent's exact permission set from the public scopeCommitment. Fix: add a private `blindingSalt` input and compute `scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt)`. Apply the same fix in Delegation.circom's `newScopeCommitment` computation. This adds ~300 constraints (one extra Poseidon input) and closes the enumeration attack.

title: Delegation circuit does not verify delegator credential is enrolled
description: The Delegation circuit takes `delegatorCredCommitment` as a private input and uses it to recompute the previous scope commitment, but never proves that `delegatorCredCommitment` is a leaf in any Merkle tree. A malicious prover can fabricate a delegatorCredCommitment that satisfies `Poseidon(delegatorScope, delegatorCredCommitment) == previousScopeCommitment` without the delegator ever having been enrolled. The on-chain contract checks `previousScopeCommitment` chain-linking but that only proves consistency with a prior proof's output — if the first hop's scope commitment was constructed with a phantom credential, the entire chain is tainted. Fix: add a delegator Merkle inclusion proof (identical pattern to the existing delegatee inclusion check) and expose `delegatorMerkleRoot` as a public output verified on-chain against agentRootHistory.

title: HumanUniqueness nullifier is scope-bound but scope is static — enables cross-session tracking
description: HumanUniqueness computes `nullifierHash = Poseidon(scope, secret)`. The scope for handshakes is described as a constant (e.g., hash('bolyra-handshake-v1')). This means the same human produces the same nullifier in EVERY handshake session, making them linkable across sessions despite the ZKP privacy goal. The sessionNonce is bound via a separate `nonceBinding` output but the nullifier itself is deterministic. An on-chain observer (or any verifier who sees two handshakes) can link them to the same human. Fix: include sessionNonce in the nullifier derivation — `nullifierHash = Poseidon(scope, secret, sessionNonce)` — making nullifiers unique per session. Sybil resistance (one-proof-per-scope) can be enforced application-side via the nonceBinding output rather than the nullifier.

title: Agent/Delegation timestamp liveness check window is unspecified — potential for stale proof replay
description: IdentityRegistry has `AgentTimestampStale` and `DelegationTimestampStale` error types, implying an on-chain check that `currentTimestamp` (a public input set by the prover) is close to `block.timestamp`. However, the acceptable window is critical: too wide (e.g., 1 hour) and an attacker can pre-generate proofs and submit them later; too narrow and legitimate proofs fail due to block inclusion delay. The contract should define an explicit constant (e.g., `MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT = 300 seconds`), document the threat model, and the circuit should enforce `expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp` via a LessThan comparator (which AgentPolicy has, but the exact bound should be auditable). Fix: add `uint256 public constant MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT = 300;` and require `block.timestamp - pubSignals.currentTimestamp <= MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT` with a clear NatSpec comment explaining the security rationale.

title: Typed error hierarchy with machine-readable codes
description: The SDK and contracts throw bare `Error` strings (e.g. `throw new Error('Missing or invalid proof')`) with no structured code, making programmatic error handling impossible. Implement a `BolyraError` base class with a `code` enum (`STALE_ROOT`, `EXPIRED_CREDENTIAL`, `SCOPE_MISMATCH`, `ARTIFACT_NOT_FOUND`, `NONCE_REUSED`, etc.), a human-readable `message`, and an optional `cause`. Map every Solidity custom error to its SDK counterpart so `catch (e) { if (e.code === 'STALE_ROOT') retry() }` works. This directly unblocks LangChain/CrewAI integrations that need to distinguish retryable from fatal errors.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder with compile-time scope narrowing
description: Delegation currently requires manually constructing witness inputs, computing scope commitments, and chaining hops by hand — the experiment stub is empty. Build a `DelegationBuilder` with a fluent API: `bolyra.delegate(agentCred).narrowScope(Permissions.READ_DATA | Permissions.WRITE_DATA).expireBefore(parentExpiry).to(delegateeCredCommitment).prove()`. The builder validates cumulative-bit invariants at construction time (TypeScript type-level where possible), auto-chains `previousScopeCommitment` across hops, and refuses to build if scope widens. This makes delegation usable without understanding circuit internals.

title: Self-describing proof envelope for handshake results
description: The `HandshakeResult.humanProof` and `agentProof` are typed as `unknown`, making serialization, transport, and debugging opaque. Define a `ProofEnvelope` type with fields `circuitId`, `provingSystem`, `proof` (hex), `publicSignals` (string[]), `metadata` (timestamp, sdkVersion). Add `toJSON()`/`fromJSON()` round-trip methods and a `toCBOR()`/`fromCBOR()` pair for wire transport (aligning with the proof-envelope content-type experiment). This lets developers serialize proofs to HTTP headers, store them in databases, and debug them without reverse-engineering internal formats.

title: Named permission constants with helper utilities
description: The 8-bit cumulative permission bitmask is documented only in CLAUDE.md and circuit comments — SDK consumers must manually construct numeric bitmasks and remember implication rules (bit 4 implies bits 2+3). Export a `Permissions` object with named constants (`Permissions.READ_DATA`, `Permissions.FINANCIAL_SMALL`, etc.), a `Permissions.combine(...perms)` helper that auto-sets implied bits, a `Permissions.validate(mask)` that throws on invalid cumulative encoding, and a `Permissions.describe(mask)` for human-readable debugging output. This prevents the #1 likely integration bug.

title: Offline verification mode (no provider required)
description: Currently `BolyraClient` requires an ethers/viem provider even for local development and testing, because it fetches Merkle proofs on-chain. Add a `BolyraClient.offline({ artifacts })` factory that accepts a local in-memory Merkle tree (or pre-computed Merkle proof), skips on-chain lookups, and performs snarkjs-only verification. This lets developers build and test integrations without a blockchain connection, dramatically reducing the hello-world friction from 'deploy contracts + run node + fund wallet' to 'npm install + 5 lines of code'.
